items.prompt: What does an amortized O(1) operation guarantee?

options.text: For every allowed operation sequence, total cost divided across the sequence is O(1) per operation, even if some individual operations are expensive.
Every individual operation has worst-case O(1) cost.
The expected cost is O(1) only under a random input distribution.
Only the first operation is O(1).

feedback.reason: Amortized analysis spreads infrequent expensive operations across many cheap operations.
feedback.details: It is a deterministic sequence guarantee, not a promise that each call is cheap.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.each_worst_constant: An occasional operation may be much more expensive.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.random_average: That describes average or expected analysis, not deterministic amortized analysis.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.first_only: The claim concerns the sequence total.

items.prompt: How does amortized analysis differ from average-case analysis?

options.text: Amortized analysis does not require a probability distribution over inputs or operations.
They are identical names for the same concept.
Amortized analysis is the worst-case cost of one operation.
Amortized analysis applies only to output size.

feedback.reason: Average-case reasoning asks for an expectation under a model.
feedback.details: Amortized reasoning bounds the total cost of any valid sequence and divides it across operations.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.same: Average case depends on a distribution; amortized analysis can hold for every sequence.

items.prompt: A dynamic array doubles capacity when full. What are the worst-case and amortized costs of one append under the fixed-element-copy model?

options.text: Worst-case O(n), amortized O(1).
Both worst-case and amortized O(1).
Both O(n).
Worst-case O(1), amortized O(n).

feedback.reason: An append that triggers resizing copies the current contents.
feedback.details: Geometric capacity growth makes the sum of all copies across n appends linear, so average charged cost per append is constant.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.both_1: A resize may copy n existing elements.

items.prompt: Why is geometric capacity growth important for dynamic-array amortized append?

options.text: Capacity increases by a constant factor, so expensive copies occur after increasingly many cheap appends.
It eliminates copying entirely.
It makes resize positions random.
The variable capacity has a special Big-O meaning.

feedback.reason: Doubling produces copy sizes 1, 2, 4, 8, and so on.
feedback.details: The geometric sum remains proportional to the final number of elements.

items.prompt: A stack supports push, pop, and multipop(k), where multipop removes at most k existing items. Why can multipop be amortized O(1) over a sequence?

options.text: Each pushed item can be popped at most once before it would need to be pushed again.
Every multipop call removes at most one item.
The stack remains sorted.
k is chosen randomly.

feedback.reason: The total number of successful pops is bounded by the total number of pushes.
feedback.details: Even if one multipop is expensive, all removals across m operations are O(m).

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.multipop_constant: One call can remove many items.

items.prompt: A binary counter increments by flipping trailing ones to zero and the next zero to one. Why is increment amortized O(1) bit flips?

options.text: Higher bits flip exponentially less often, so total flips over n increments are O(n).
Every increment flips exactly one bit.
The claim holds only because the counter width is one bit.
Bit patterns are assumed random.

feedback.reason: Bit 0 flips every increment, bit 1 every two increments, bit 2 every four, and so on.
feedback.details: Summing those frequencies gives fewer than 2n flips over n increments.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.every_increment_one_flip: Values ending in ones flip multiple bits.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.counter_width_constant_only: The argument handles growing bit length.

items.prompt: Can an amortized O(1) operation still cause a visible latency spike in one call?

options.text: Yes. Amortized analysis permits occasional expensive individual operations.
No. Amortized O(1) means strict worst-case O(1).
Only under random inputs.
Only when output size is large.

feedback.reason: Amortized throughput and per-operation latency are different contracts.
feedback.details: Real-time systems may require worst-case bounds even when total sequence cost is excellent.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.no: That confuses amortized and worst-case guarantees.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.only_random: The expensive event can be deterministic.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.only_output: Resizing or cleanup can cause the spike without large output.

items.prompt: Is the statement "an operation is usually cheap" sufficient to prove a good amortized bound?

options.text: No. The frequency and total cost of expensive operations must be bounded over every valid sequence.
Yes. Informal frequency is enough.
Yes, because amortized means probabilistic.
Only the operation name is needed.

feedback.reason: A proof must charge every expensive event to a limited resource, credit, or potential decrease.
feedback.details: Without a sequence bound, 'usually' is only an observation.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.yes_usually: A rare operation can be catastrophically expensive.

items.prompt: A data structure advertises amortized O(1) insertion. Which statement is safe?

options.text: A sequence of n insertions has O(n) total insertion cost under the stated model.
Every insertion individually takes O(1) worst-case time.
The guarantee holds only in expectation over random insertions.
Any preprocessing cost can be ignored.

feedback.reason: Multiplying the amortized bound by the sequence length yields the total bound.
feedback.details: The distribution of cost across individual insertions may be uneven.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.each_constant: The amortized claim does not guarantee this.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.expected_only: That would need an average-case or expected-time statement.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.preprocessing_free: The sequence model must specify all charged work.

items.prompt: Which statements correctly distinguish amortized and worst-case cost? Select all that apply.

options.text: Worst-case operation cost bounds the most expensive individual call.
Amortized cost bounds total cost across a sequence.
Amortized O(1) proves every call is O(1).
Both claims require a defined operation and cost model.

feedback.reason: Worst-case and amortized claims answer different performance questions.
feedback.details: They can coexist: an operation may be worst-case O(n) and amortized O(1).

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.worst_single: It is a per-operation latency guarantee.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.amortized_sequence: Expensive calls can be spread across cheap calls.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.both_use_model: The charged work must be explicit.

items.prompt: Which facts support the dynamic-array amortized append proof? Select all that apply.

options.text: Capacity grows by a constant factor greater than one.
A resize copies the current number of stored elements.
The total copied elements across growth steps form a geometric sum O(n).
Capacity increases by exactly one on every full append.

feedback.reason: The proof depends on both the resize cost and how often it occurs.
feedback.details: Growing by one would destroy the constant amortized bound.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.grow_one: That would cause 1 + 2 + ... + n copying, which is quadratic.

items.prompt: Which methods can establish an amortized bound? Select all that apply.

options.text: Bound total cost of n operations and divide by n.
Charge extra credit to cheap operations to pay for later expensive ones.
Track stored potential whose change offsets actual cost.
Measure a random subset and call the average amortized.

feedback.reason: Amortized proofs can be presented through total cost, credits, or potential.
feedback.details: All three are deterministic accounting frameworks.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.random_sample: That is empirical average-case evidence, not a proof.

items.prompt: Which claims are invalid uses of amortized analysis? Select all that apply.

options.text: A real-time requirement can ignore a worst-case O(n) resize because append is amortized O(1).
A measured mean over random inputs is automatically an amortized proof.
An unbounded cleanup is harmless merely because it occurs rarely, without bounding rarity or cost.
n amortized-O(1) operations have O(n) total charged cost.

feedback.reason: Amortized analysis cannot replace latency guarantees or unproven frequency assumptions.
feedback.details: Every expensive event must be accounted for.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.ignore_spike: Real-time latency may require per-operation bounds.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.random_average: A distributional sample is not a sequence guarantee.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.unbounded_cleanup: Total sequence cost may still be large.

elements.text: Define an arbitrary sequence of n allowed operations.
Bound the total actual cost of the whole sequence.
Divide the total bound by n.
State the amortized result and any separate worst-case operation cost.

feedback.reason: The aggregate method proves a sequence guarantee directly.
feedback.details: Separately stating worst-case latency prevents the common equality mistake.

items.prompt: Arrange an accounting-method explanation for dynamic-array append.

elements.text: Charge each cheap append a constant amount greater than its immediate cost.
Use accumulated credit to pay for the next resize copy.
Conclude a constant amortized charge covers every append.

feedback.reason: Credits move cost from rare expensive operations to preceding cheap operations.
feedback.details: The proof must show credits never become negative.

items.prompt: A dynamic array starts empty and doubles capacity. Across n appends, select total append cost, amortized cost per append, and worst-case cost of one append.

feedback.reason: Geometric resize copies sum to O(n) across n appends.
feedback.details: One append can still copy the current array, so its individual worst case is linear.

items.prompt: A stack executes m operations chosen from push, pop, and multipop. Each pushed item can be removed at most once before a later push. Select total cost and amortized cost per operation.
constraints: Each successful push or pop costs O(1).

feedback.reason: There can be at most m pushes and at most one successful pop per pushed item.
feedback.details: A single multipop may be large, but total removals remain linear in the sequence length.

items.prompt: A binary counter starts at zero and is incremented n times. Select total bit flips, amortized flips per increment, and worst-case flips of one increment.
constraints: The counter has enough bits to represent values through n.

feedback.reason: Each bit flips at a geometrically decreasing frequency, so total flips are linear.
feedback.details: An increment after a run of trailing ones can flip every represented bit.
